title: Applying external masonry paint
subtitle: Prepare and paint a rendered or brick exterior wall
excerpt: How to prepare and paint an outside wall: clearing loose material, stabilising powdery render, applying a masonry stabiliser, and rolling or brushing two coats of exterior masonry paint for a weatherproof, long-lasting finish.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s).

content.text: Exterior masonry paint protects render and brick from frost, rain, and UV damage. The job is one of preparation and application in the correct conditions: paint applied to a wet, dirty, or unstable surface will peel within the first winter. The preparation takes as long as the painting; this is not a job to begin if rain is forecast or the temperature is below 5 degrees.
When not to start
Rain forecast within 24 hours. Exterior paint needs to form a skin before it is wetted; rain on fresh paint washes pigment from the surface and leaves tide marks.
Temperature below 5 degrees Celsius or above 30 degrees. Below 5 degrees, acrylic masonry paint does not cure properly. Above 30 degrees, it skins too fast and brush-marks remain.
Direct strong sunlight on the face to be painted. Paint a wall in shade or in the morning before the sun reaches it.
Step 1, prepare the surface

Scrub the wall with a stiff brush to remove loose paint, dirt, algae, and moss. A pressure washer on a medium setting removes algae and old paint quickly on smooth render; work from the top down. Allow the wall to dry thoroughly: at least 3 days after wet weather in a warm period, longer in autumn and winter. Brush loose paint and any sandy areas with the hand (powdery surfaces mean the previous paint is failing and the render below is breaking down).

Repair any cracks with an exterior filler or a flexible exterior caulk for gaps wider than 2 mm. Repair spalled or delaminating render with sand-and-cement mortar (1 part cement to 4 parts sharp sand). Allow repairs to cure for at least 2 weeks before painting.
Step 2, apply masonry stabiliser
Apply
masonry stabiliser

to any powdery, chalky, or previously unpainted areas using a large brush or roller. Allow to dry for the time on the label (typically 4 hours in good conditions). On previously painted walls in good condition, skip this step if the surface is sound.
Step 3, protect doors, windows, and paving

Mask window frames and door frames with low-tack tape. Lay dust sheets or polythene over paving directly below the wall: masonry paint, once dry on paving, requires a masonry cleaner to remove. Masonry paint drips from a loaded brush more than interior paint; work with a cloth tucked into a pocket for immediate wipe-downs.
Step 4, apply the first coat
Stir the masonry paint thoroughly; aggregate sinks to the bottom. For
textured masonry paint

, use a thick-pile roller sleeve (15 mm nap) for the main wall area and a 75 mm brush for edges and cut-in work. For smooth masonry paint, a medium-pile sleeve (10 mm) gives good coverage without excessive stippling. Cut in around windows and doors with the brush first, then roll the main wall area. Work in sections from top to bottom and maintain a wet edge across the full width of each section.
Step 5, second coat

Allow the first coat to dry for at least 4 hours. Apply the second coat, working in the same direction. Two coats of good-quality exterior masonry paint provide a service life of 8 to 12 years on a well-prepared surface. Attempting a one-coat job to save time produces a finish that fails within 3 years.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Remove all loose paint by scraping and pressure-washing. Allow the wall to dry for a minimum of 1 week in dry weather. Re-apply from stabiliser stage.
attrs.cause: Surface was not fully dry before painting, or the wall was painted in cold or damp conditions.
attrs.symptom: Paint peels in large sections within the first year

attrs.fix: Paint in shaded or overcast conditions. Keep the roller or brush moving; do not attempt to brush out a surface that has already skinned.
attrs.cause: Paint applied in direct sunlight or wind, or applied too slowly over a skinning surface.
attrs.symptom: Brush marks visible in the dried finish

attrs.fix: Treat the surface with a diluted exterior fungicidal wash, allow to dry, and repaint. Consider a masonry paint with a built-in biocide for north-facing walls.
attrs.cause: Surface not treated with a biocide or fungicidal wash before painting, or the wall faces north and is chronically damp.
attrs.symptom: Algae or green staining returns within a season
